titleStmt.title: Letter from John R. Moorhead to Sir Robert Chalmers, 2 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: John R. Moorhead

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from John R. Moorhead (1859-1936), the crown solicitor for Belfast, to the under-secretary for Ireland, Sir Robert Chalmers (1858-1938). This letter refers to the case of Mrs Morgan, the wife of the deceased Private Morgan. Mrs Morgan was convicted for obtaining a large over payment of separation allowance and the letter concerns Moorhead's difficulties in getting Mrs Morgan to repay the amount she 'wrongfully obtained'. He believes that it is better to receive the restitution, even if it is delayed, rather than impose a sentence which would put an end to Mrs Morgan's repayments completely. Enclosed with the letter is a statement detailing the repayments Mrs Moran has already made.Sir Robert Chalmers was sent to Ireland as replacement under-secretary for Sir Matthew Nathan, who had resigned along with Augustine Birrell, his chief secretary, following the outbreak of the Easter Rising in 1916.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/9392/1

correspAction.placeName: 30 Rosemary Street, Belfast, Co. Antrim, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Counties of Antrim & Belfast Crown Solicitor's Office 30 Rosemary Street. Belfast.
MOORHEAD & WOOD. Solicitors 2nd June 1916
JOHN R. MOORHEAD Crown Solicitor.Telephone No 605 Dear sir, Relative to the case referred to in the enclosed Memo., you will
observe that Mrs Morgan, wife of 10692 Private Morgan, late Royal Irish
Rifles, who was convicted before the Recorder of Belfast, for obtaining
a large overpayment of Separation Allowance and who was put under a rule
of bail for two years from the date of her conviction to come up for
sentence at any time within that period on getting 10 days' notice from
the Crown Solicitor and conditional that she repaid the amount wrongfully
obtained by instalments of 10/- per week, - you will observe from the
copy Return attached that Mrs Morgan has not been at all punctually making
these payments. Sometimes she omitted payment altogether and having made
the first few payments she began to reduce the amount of her instalments.
She has constantly made excuses and when reminded of the statement made
by Counsel on her behalf at the time the order was made that 'about Â£10
per week was coming into her house in the form of wages for munition work
by members of her family' she replied that that statement was quite
inaccurate and was a mere fabrication of Counsel. Up to the present I
have been in great doubt as to what to do with this and the several
similar cases in which I am collecting instalments under similar orders.
If I attempt to hold them strictly to the instalment agreed on, and the
amount is not paid, or not regularly paid, my only remedy is to serve
the 10-days' notice and have the sentence imposed, but the result of
that would be no more restitution. It seems to me that the obtaining
of restitution as far as possible was more important than securing
the punishment for the offender. I have been temporizing and
threatening to bring the defaulters up for sentence but have not
actually done so, but there comes a time when the instalments are
approaching vanishing point and it begins to appear doubtful whether
sharper measures should not be adopted. I should be glad if you will ascertain from the Military Authorities
what they wish me to do. My own idea, subject to approval of the
Paymaster's Department, would be, not to bring the parties up on
notice for sentence so long as there is any hope of obtaining any
further substantial payments; in other words, to continue as I have
been doing, to exercise as much pressure as possible upon them so
as to obtain the maximum amount of restitution before resorting to
the notice for sentence. I am,
Your obedt. servant, John R Moorhead
Crown Solicitor. The Under Secretary, The Castle, Dublin.
Â£11. 19. 6.
